question: [File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the world-writable /tmp directory to atomically replace /tmp/alive with a file whose mtime is exactly 2 minutes and 1 nanosecond old, reliably triggering the age > 2*time.Minute condition and causing the liveness probe to call os.Exit(1) on every invocation?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the lack of atomic file operations in the healthcheck (os.Stat is not atomic with respect to concurrent writes) to cause a race where /tmp/alive exists but has a stale mtime due to a partial write by the pinger, causing the liveness probe to fail?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit a shared /tmp volume between multiple containers in the same pod to interfere with /tmp/alive or /tmp/ready from a sidecar container, causing the healthcheck to fail and crash-loop the pinger pod?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that os.Stat('/tmp/alive') follows symlinks to create a symlink chain (/tmp/alive -> /tmp/b -> /tmp/c -> ... -> /tmp/alive) causing a symlink loop, making os.Stat() return ELOOP, which triggers os.Exit(1) on the liveness probe and crash-loops the pod?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary uses os.Exit(2) for unknown modes to confuse a Kubernetes liveness probe configured with a non-standard exit code interpretation, causing the orchestrator to misinterpret the probe result and take incorrect action on the pod?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that os.Args[1] is read without sanitization to pass a crafted argument (e.g., a very long string or a string with special characters) that causes the default case to execute and print to stderr, potentially filling the container log buffer and causing log-based resource exhaustion?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary is invoked as an exec probe (not a network probe) to repeatedly invoke it with invalid arguments, causing os.Exit(2) on every call, which Kubernetes interprets as a probe failure, triggering pod restarts in a non-network DoS?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that /tmp is a tmpfs (memory-backed) filesystem to exhaust container memory by filling /tmp with large files, causing the pinger's os.WriteFile('/tmp/alive', ...) to fail with ENOMEM or ENOSPC, making /tmp/alive go stale and the liveness probe crash-loop the pod?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary does not use file locking to create a race condition where /tmp/alive is deleted between the os.Stat() call and the fi.ModTime() call, causing a nil pointer dereference panic that results in a non-zero exit code and a liveness probe failure?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary is a statically compiled binary (CGO_ENABLED=0) running in a scratch container to bypass any runtime security controls and directly manipulate /tmp/alive or /tmp/ready via a shared volume, causing probe failures?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that the container runs as UID 1000 (USER 1000:1000 in Dockerfile) and /tmp is typically owned by root with mode 1777 (sticky bit) to create files in /tmp that the pinger (also UID 1000) cannot delete or overwrite, causing /tmp/alive to be replaced with an attacker-controlled file with a stale mtime?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the sticky bit behavior of /tmp: since the pinger runs as UID 1000, an attacker running as a different UID in the same namespace could create /tmp/alive owned by their UID, and the pinger (UID 1000) cannot delete or overwrite it, causing the heartbeat to fail and the liveness probe to crash-loop the pod?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary reads fi.ModTime() from os.Stat() which uses the filesystem's mtime, and if the container's filesystem clock differs from the host clock (e.g., due to timezone misconfiguration), time.Since(fi.ModTime()) could return an inflated value, causing false 'heartbeat stale' failures and pod crash-loops?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that the liveness probe threshold is exactly 2 minutes while the heartbeat interval is 15 seconds (from pinger/main.go), leaving only an 8x margin, to cause a DoS by introducing just enough CPU starvation (e.g., via a CPU-intensive process in the same cgroup) to delay the heartbeat goroutine beyond 2 minutes?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit cgroup CPU throttling (if they can submit workloads to the same node) to starve the pinger process's goroutine scheduler, preventing the heartbeat goroutine from executing and writing /tmp/alive within the 2-minute window, causing the liveness probe to fail and crash-loop the pod?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary is invoked as a subprocess by the Kubernetes kubelet to exhaust the process table (PID namespace exhaustion) by spawning many processes, preventing the healthcheck binary from being forked, causing the probe to fail with a system error and crash-loop the pod?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that fmt.Fprintln(os.Stderr, err) in the 'live' case prints the raw error from os.Stat(), which may include the full file path, to leak sensitive path information via container logs that could aid further attacks?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary does not validate the content of /tmp/alive (only its mtime), to create a /tmp/alive file with arbitrary content but a fresh mtime, causing the liveness probe to pass even when the pinger is not actually running, masking a real DoS condition?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary does not validate the content of /tmp/ready (only its existence), to create a /tmp/ready file before the pinger has completed initialization, causing the readiness probe to pass prematurely and route traffic to an uninitialized pod?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary uses os.Exit() directly (without deferred cleanup) to cause resource leaks if the binary is invoked in a context where file descriptors or other resources need cleanup, potentially exhausting file descriptor limits over many probe invocations?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that the mode variable defaults to 'ready' when no argument is provided (len(os.Args) <= 1), to invoke the healthcheck binary without arguments and always trigger the readiness check, potentially causing unintended readiness probe behavior if the binary is misconfigured as a liveness probe?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary is a separate binary from the pinger (built separately in the Dockerfile) to replace the /healthcheck binary in the container filesystem (if writable) with a malicious binary that always returns exit code 0, masking real liveness/readiness failures and preventing legitimate pod restarts?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary is built from a scratch base image (no shell, no utilities) to bypass filesystem-level defenses and directly manipulate /tmp/alive or /tmp/ready via a shared volume mount, causing probe failures without triggering any shell-based security controls?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary uses time.Since() which relies on the monotonic clock, and if the container's monotonic clock is reset (e.g., due to a container checkpoint/restore operation), time.Since(fi.ModTime()) could return an incorrect value, causing false liveness probe failures?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: High: Non-network-based DoS affecting projects with greater than or equal to 25% of the market capitalization on top of the respective layer'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that os.Stat('/tmp/alive') returns a FileInfo whose ModTime() uses wall clock time (not monotonic), making the liveness check vulnerable to NTP time adjustments that move the clock forward, causing time.Since(fi.ModTime()) to exceed 2 minutes and crash-loop the pod?
